summary: Gruber celebrates BBEdit's 30th anniversary by revisiting Rich Siegel's original 1992 Usenet announcement and reflecting on the text editor's remarkable longevity. He highlights how BBEdit's original features — multi-file search, grep support, and freedom from the 32K file size limit — were groundbreaking at the time and remain core strengths today. Gruber shares his personal history with the app, including working at Bare Bones Software and creating Markdown in BBEdit specifically for use with BBEdit. He rounds up tributes from fellow long-time users who praise the app's consistency, quality, and faithfulness to its original design principles.
key_points: BBEdit's 1992 debut broke through platform limitations like the 32K file size cap and offered grep pattern-matching that was revolutionary for a Mac text editor
Gruber's personal connection to BBEdit spans three decades, including working at Bare Bones and creating Markdown as a BBEdit-native tool
BBEdit version 14.1 remains 'remarkably similar in spirit' to the original, a rare achievement in software longevity
The post serves as a curated tribute, gathering praise from respected Mac community voices who all emphasize BBEdit's consistency and reliability over decades
money_quotes: BBEdit today (version 14.1, released a month ago) is remarkably similar in spirit to BBEdit then.
BBEdit's multi-file search and replace remains the best I've ever seen. In 1992, though, it was a breakthrough.
And I remember thinking, 'Grep search, what's that about?'
18 years ago I created Markdown in BBEdit, with the intention of using it from BBEdit. That's worked out pretty well.
Just about every long piece I've written for Daring Fireball was written in BBEdit (including this one, natch).
I can think of no other piece of software that has stayed so true to its original design principles as BBEdit.
key_insight: BBEdit's 30-year run demonstrates that software can endure and thrive by staying true to its original design principles rather than chasing trends.
